takeaway: Productboard frames discovery via Cagan's two questions — "you need to discover whether there are real users out there that want this product" and then "a product solution to this problem that is usable, useful, and feasible." The purpose is risk reduction: "We conduct product discovery because we want to reduce the risks around what we decide to build," and discovery precedes and complements delivery — teams "research and validate concepts before sending them to engineering" rather than validating after launch.

Marty Cagan's four big risks, the canonical map of what discovery must attack: value risk ("Will customers buy it or users choose to use it"), usability risk ("Can users figure out how to use it"), feasibility risk ("Can engineering build it with the available time, skills, and tech"), and business viability risk ("Does it work for legal, finance, sales, marketing, and brand"). The discipline is that "Discovery exists to kill all four before delivery starts" — "deliberately attacking all four before you commit engineering capacity."

Teresa Torres's Continuous Discovery Habits reframes discovery as an ongoing team habit — "continuously infuse their product decisions with customer input" in a "structured, and sustainable way" — rather than a phase. Her sharpest correction of research intent: "The purpose of interviewing is NOT to ask what to build. It's to discover and explore opportunities." And the economic case: "Agile helped teams build more software - but agile doesn't help us build the right thing. The fruit of discovery work is the time saved not building something that won't work."

LogRocket marks the generational shift in vocabulary: "Continuous discovery has essentially replaced dual-track agile over the last couple of years," because the two-track metaphor understated how tightly the work interleaves — "Discovery and delivery are intertwined" and "discovery feeds delivery and delivery feeds discovery." The shared goal is de-risking: continuous discovery "aims to help product teams build the right things right and quickly test assumptions to reduce waste and mitigate the risk of building the wrong thing."

Dual-track agile "integrates the activities of product discovery and delivery into two synchronized tracks" run concurrently: the discovery track pinpoints "user issues, pain points and problems, and performing experiments to validate your potential solutions," while the delivery track handles "the actual creation and refinement of the product based on validated insights from the Discovery track." Origin: it "originated from the collaborative efforts of" Jeff Patton and Marty Cagan, with Patton introducing it in 2007 (building on Desiree Sy's Autodesk work).

Teresa Torres's opportunity solution tree is "a simple way of visually representing the paths you might take to reach a desired outcome." Its hierarchy is load-bearing: "The root of the tree is your desired outcome... Next is the opportunity space... Below the opportunity space is the solution space... Below the solution space are assumption tests." It exists to keep product trios honest — "Build and maintain a shared understanding of how they might reach their desired outcome" and "Unlock faster learning cycles" — by forcing solutions to attach to a validated opportunity.

Product School details the OST's four levels: the Outcome is "the ultimate goal... clear, measurable, and focused on delivering value"; Opportunities "represent unmet customer needs or pain points that, if addressed, could move the team closer"; Solutions "are the ideas or features that the team believes will address the identified opportunities"; and Experiments "are tests that help validate whether a proposed solution is effective." The structural payoff: "every experiment is retrofitted to a specific goal," preventing premature solution-jumping by requiring opportunities be found through research first.

The generative/evaluative split mapped onto Nielsen Norman's design cycle: generative research "refers to the methods your team implements in the Discover and Explore stages... It seeks to uncover a deeper understanding of your users to identify opportunities for more valuable solutions" (field studies, interviews, diary studies, journey mapping). Evaluative research "analyzes potential usability issues to validate that your product is aligned with real users... in the Test and Listen stages" (usability testing, benchmark testing, surveys). Generative finds the problem; evaluative checks the solution.

Codecademy sharpens the distinction by intent: generative research is for "developing a deeper understanding of your users and defining the problem" where "the world is your oyster," and it explores "if there even is a problem to solve." Evaluative research is to "assess whether a product or concept meets expectations and requirements" and requires "something tangible to evaluate and test, like an existing design or prototype." The cleanest framing: generative is "more open-ended" while evaluative "has a defined objective."

Steve Blank's customer development is the discovery substrate the product canon inherits: "customer development is done almost entirely outside the building in front of customers and partners," and it "really is a formal process of how you test all of your hypotheses." The founder's role is explicitly learning, not execution — "a founder's job whose job is learning and discovery" — and the famous discipline is that there are no facts inside the building, so validation must happen in front of real customers.

David Bland's assumptions mapping is "a team exercise where desirability, viability, and feasibility hypotheses are made explicit and prioritized in terms of importance and evidence." It plots assumptions on a 2x2 — "The x-axis measures Evidence (have evidence/no evidence), while the y-axis measures Importance" — and the action is to test the top-right quadrant first: "beliefs that are critical for success and yet have the least amount of evidence to support them. You'll want your teams to focus their near term experimentation here."

Jeff Gothelf and Josh Seiden's Lean UX reframes product work as testable assumptions — the canvas has you "declaring assumptions as we work through" it. The hypothesis statement gives structure: "We believe that this business outcome will be achieved if this user attains this benefit with this feature," connecting metric, segment, benefit, and solution into one falsifiable claim. The governing question is economy of learning: "What's the least amount of work we need to do to learn the next most important thing?"

Strategyzer's reading of Eric Ries's build-measure-learn loop, which sits "at the heart of the Lean Startup process," stresses that the MVP "is not necessarily the smallest product imaginable... it is simply the fastest way to get through the Build-Measure-Learn feedback loop with the minimum amount of effort." The counterintuitive lesson for discovery: "There are endless ways to test your assumptions before actually building anything," because "the goal is not to start building a limited version... The goal is to achieve maximum learning for the least amount of effort."

The Riskiest Assumption Test (RAT) "involves rapidly identifying and testing the riskiest assumptions and potential fail points for any initiative early in the process." It contrasts with the MVP: "the MVP... tends to focus on previewing strengths and building on success, while the RAT is all about testing weaknesses upfront," using "learn-test" cycles before building. In practice a RAT "will often take the form of targeted experiments such as a landing page, survey, or narrow prototype, rather than anything resembling the product itself" — the cheapest possible test of the highest-risk belief.

Melissa Perri's build trap is the organizational failure discovery exists to prevent: teams "defining and shipping software with no measures of success... focusing on getting features out the door," "too focused on building any thing rather than the right thing." The escape is structural, not personal: "we need to create a customer-centric organisation that rewards learning and reaching outcomes, rather than focusing on outputs" — making discovery the engine that converts the outcome mandate into validated bets.

The most common discovery pitfall is confirmation bias — "Looking for evidence that proves you right, and avoiding or ignoring evidence that contradicts your beliefs" — compounded by the fact that "not enough people talk about success criteria, or detail how it is they'll know when they've solved the problem." The framing warning: "you might have a groundbreaking idea, but without proper discovery, you won't know if it's the right one for your users" — discovery's value is in the disconfirmation it makes possible, not the confirmation teams instinctively seek.
